---
title: "Harmonized Proteomics Analysis Protocol"
author: "Protocol for OpenMS, DIA-NN, MaxQuant, and Spectronaut"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
---

# Overview

This protocol describes a **harmonized analysis workflow** for running **OpenMS, DIA-NN, MaxQuant, and Spectronaut** on the same proteomics dataset while keeping **core parameters as consistent as possible**.\
The goal is to minimize configuration-induced bias so that observed differences primarily reflect algorithmic design.

> **Important note:** Exact equivalence between tools is not possible due to 
fundamentally different scoring, inference, and machine-learning strategies. 
This protocol defines *core parameters* that are conceptually shared.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Experimental Assumptions

-   Same raw MS files are used across all tools
-   Same FASTA file for database search (Please download it before 4th May vie 
this link https://filesender.funet.fi/?s=download&token=f8310a8c-2fa0-472a-8adc-5231917c058c)
-   Same acquisition strategy (DIA)
-   Same biological samples and replicates
-   Same level of comparison (primarily **peptide-level**)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Core Parameters

## 1. Input Data and Global Settings

| Parameter            | Harmonized Value      | Notes                         |
|----------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Raw files            | Same vendor RAW files | No mixing of file versions    |
| Centroiding          | Vendor centroiding    | Avoid profile/centroid mixing |
| Acquisition type     | DIA                   | Must be identical             |
| Instrument model     | Orbitrap Astral       | Conceptual matching           |
| Internal calibration | Enabled               | In all tools                  |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. Database and Digestion

| Parameter          | Harmonized Value     |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| FASTA database     | Identical FASTA file |
| Contaminants       | Same contaminant set |
| Decoy strategy     | Reverse target–decoy |
| Enzyme             | Trypsin/P            |
| Missed cleavages   | 2                    |
| Enzyme specificity | Fully tryptic        |
| Min peptide length | 7 amino acids        |
| Max peptide length | 30–40 amino acids    |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. Modifications

| Parameter                   | Harmonized Value                    |
|-----------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Fixed modifications         | Carbamidomethyl (C)                 |
| Variable modifications      | Oxidation (M), Acetylation (N-term) |
| Protein N-term acetylation  | Enabled                             |
| Max variable mods / peptide | 5                                   |
| Open modification search    | Disabled                            |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4. Mass Accuracy and Identification

| Parameter | Harmonized Value | Rationale |
|-------------------|---------------------------------|---------------------|
| Precursor tolerance | Orbitrap-appropriate (≈10–20 ppm) | Conceptual equivalence |
| Fragment tolerance | Orbitrap-appropriate (≈20–30 ppm) | Do not force identical numbers |
| MS1 recalibration | Enabled | Improve mass accuracy |
| MS2 recalibration | Enabled | Improve scoring |
| Identification scoring | Tool-specific | Defaults retained |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5. FDR Control and Quantification

| Parameter            | Harmonized Value        |
|----------------------|-------------------------|
| PSM FDR              | 1%                      |
| Peptide FDR          | 1%                      |
| Protein FDR          | 1%                      |
| Quantification level | MS2-based               |
| Match-between-runs   | On or Off (same choice) |
| Normalization        | Global / median-based   |

> **Recommendation:** Primary comparisons should be performed at the **peptide level**. Protein-level results should be treated as secondary due to differing inference models.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# DIA-Specific Settings (If Applicable)

| Parameter         | Harmonized Value                             |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| Analysis mode     | Library-free (library prediction from FASTA) |
| Spectral library  | Same library file if used                    |
| iRT peptides      | Same iRT set (if used)                       |
| Isolation windows | Same DIA scheme                              |

Do **not** mix library-free and library-based modes across tools in a single comparison.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Tool-Specific Execution Notes

## OpenMS

-   Use equivalent digestion and modification settings
-   Enable internal calibration
-   Export peptide-level results for downstream harmonization

## DIA-NN

-   Use identical FASTA and digestion rules
-   Disable extra PTMs and heuristic expansions
-   Keep default neural-network scoring

## MaxQuant

-   Use Andromeda defaults except where harmonized
-   Disable extra variable modifications
-   Carefully document match-between-runs setting

## Spectronaut

-   Use DirectDIA for library-free analysis if applicable
-   Align digestion, modifications, and FDR thresholds

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Post-processing and Reporting

1.  Export **peptide-level tables** from all tools
2.  Apply identical filtering criteria
3.  Optionally re-aggregate to protein level externally
4.  Report overlap, sensitivity, and quantitative precision

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Limitations

-   Machine-learning models differ substantially across tools
-   Protein inference strategies are not equivalent
-   Interference correction and scoring models cannot be harmonized

These limitations should be explicitly acknowledged in comparative studies.
